title: NAC GP Modeling
text:
This report picks up where nac.qmd’s Analysis Region / OHC Anomaly sections leave off, without re-running any of that report’s plotting or re-deriving its climatology. nac.qmd publishes the ANALYSIS_BOUNDS-restricted, multi-year truth field and the truth-vs-climatology anomaly cells to data/ohc_truth/; loading those directly (~1s) skips both the expensive depth-integration step (cached separately, per year, as data/ohc_truth/truth_ohc_<year>.nc) and the RG climatology reprocessing, neither of which this report needs to repeat.


Setup — imports and experiment configuration
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import ohc
import ohc_bias
import ohc_climatology as ohc_clim

YEARS = [2020, 2021, 2022]

ANALYSIS_BOUNDS = (36, 40, -68, -62)
EXTENT_WIDE     = [-76, -47, 28, 50]
OHC_TRUTH_DIR   = "../data/ohc_truth"
DEPTHS          = ["ohc_700", "ohc_2000"]




Load nac.qmd’s published truth field and truth-vs-climatology anomaly
truth_field_all = xr.open_dataset(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_field_analysis.nc").load()
truth_anom_cells_all = pd.read_csv(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_anom_cells_all.csv", parse_dates=["month"])

title: NAC GP Modeling
text:
3.1 Background
We interpolate real Argo float OHC (ohc_700/ohc_2000 directly, not their anomaly relative to climatology) onto the full analysis-domain grid at the native 1/12\(^\circ\) resolution, using a Gaussian process fit with GpGp. Each real float profile is assigned the GLORYS truth OHC at its position and date.
code/ohc_gp_fit.R fits one GP per depth (0-700m, 0-2000m) with the stationary spatiotemporal covariance function matern_spheretime, pooling all profiles across all three years, with covariance parameters \(\theta\) = (\(\sigma^2\), \(\rho_s\), \(\rho_t\), \(\nu\), \(\tau^2\)) = (variance, spatial range, temporal range, smoothness, nugget). Smoothness is fixed at \(\nu = \tfrac{1}{2}\), where the Matérn form reduces to the simple exponential \(k(r) = e^{-r}\).
The temporal coordinate is a continuous day count (days since 2020-01-01), not day-of-year – so the fitted temporal range can extend across years rather than being capped at within-year scale by an annual wraparound, letting the model express long-range temporal autocorrelation if the data support it.
Fitting and prediction are separate scripts, so one fit is reused across both prediction grids below. code/ohc_gp_predict.R predicts via a custom Vecchia kriging step (kriging_predict) that gives both a mean and a standard error – GpGp::predictions() alone is faster but has no SE. Predicting on the coarse 1° grid (35 points) conditions each point on every one of the ~3,450 pooled profiles (“exact” mode) – cheap at that grid size, and matches the reference. Predicting on the native 1/12° grid (~3,600 points x 36 months x 2 depths) with that same full conditioning does not scale – so that pass instead uses a bounded Vecchia neighbourhood (m = 30, matching the fit’s own approximation), which reuses the same underlying machinery but conditions each point on only its 30 nearest profiles.

title: NAC GP Modeling
text:
3.2 Model Fit


Sample GLORYS truth OHC at each real Argo profile’s position/date
_dates = xr.DataArray(pd.to_datetime(argo_a["date"]).values, dims="profile")
_lats  = xr.DataArray(argo_a["lat"].values, dims="profile")
_lons  = xr.DataArray(argo_a["lon"].values, dims="profile")
_truth_at_real = truth_field_all.interp(time=_dates, latitude=_lats, longitude=_lons, method="nearest")

real_argo_direct = argo_a[["lat", "lon", "date"]].copy()
for _d in DEPTHS:
    real_argo_direct[_d] = _truth_at_real[_d].values




Fit the pooled GP once, then predict on the 1\(^\circ\) (exact) and native 1/12\(^\circ\) (bounded Vecchia) grids
_GP_DIR = "../data/gp_interp"
os.makedirs(_GP_DIR, exist_ok=True)
_real_profiles_csv    = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_profiles_real_direct.csv"
_real_fit_summary_csv = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_direct_fit_summary.csv"
_real_model_cache     = f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_direct_model_cache.rds"

_keys = ["month", "cell_lat", "cell_lon"]
_GP_MONTH_DAY = "middle"
_GP_RES_LABELS = {1 / 12: "1/12$^\circ$ (native)", 1.0: "1$^\circ$"}
# Full conditioning ("exact") is fine at 35 points; the ~3,600-point native
# grid needs the bounded Vecchia approximation to stay fast -- see Background.
_GP_PREDICT_M = {1 / 12: 30, 1.0: "exact"}
_pred_grid_csvs = {deg: f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_pred_grid_{deg:.4g}deg.csv" for deg in [1 / 12, 1.0]}
_real_out_csvs  = {deg: f"{_GP_DIR}/nac_gp_real_predictions_{deg:.4g}deg.csv" for deg in [1 / 12, 1.0]}

def _grid_lats_lons(deg):
    """Prediction grid for one resolution -- native truth grid, or a deg-degree coarsening of it."""
    if deg < 0.1:
        return truth_field_all["latitude"].values, truth_field_all["longitude"].values
    _tc = ohc.coarsen_truth(truth_field_all, deg=deg)
    return np.sort(_tc["cell_lat"].unique()), np.sort(_tc["cell_lon"].unique())

for deg, grid_csv in _pred_grid_csvs.items():
    lats, lons = _grid_lats_lons(deg)
    ohc_clim.build_pred_grid(lats, lons).to_csv(grid_csv, index=False)

ohc_clim.write_profile_csv(real_argo_direct, _real_profiles_csv, suffix="")
# Fit + predict are the expensive steps (pooled GP fit, bounded-Vecchia predict
# on the ~3,600-point native grid); skip both if the fit summary and model
# cache are already on disk. Delete both files to force a re-fit/re-predict.
if not (os.path.exists(_real_fit_summary_csv) and os.path.exists(_real_model_cache)):
    ohc_clim.run_gp_fit(_real_profiles_csv, _real_fit_summary_csv, _real_model_cache)
    for deg, out_csv in _real_out_csvs.items():
        ohc_clim.run_gp_predict(
            _real_profiles_csv, _pred_grid_csvs[deg], _real_model_cache, out_csv,
            m=_GP_PREDICT_M[deg], month_day=_GP_MONTH_DAY,
        )

gp_fit_summary = ohc_clim.load_gp_fit_summary(_real_fit_summary_csv)
gp_fit_summary.groupby("depth")[["loglik", "converged", "n_obs"]].first()

Load the GP-predicted OHC field directly (no climatology to add back)
def build_gp_res_ctx(out_csv, deg, profiles_df=None):
    """Load GP predictions from out_csv and assemble the display context for resolution deg.

    Modeling OHC directly (suffix="" throughout) means the GP's prediction *is*
    the field -- no per-year climatology reassembly needed here, unlike the
    anomaly-modeling case.
    """
    lats, lons = _grid_lats_lons(deg)
    gp_field = ohc_clim.load_gp_anomaly_field(out_csv, lats, lons, suffix="")

    cells    = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(gp_field)
    se_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(gp_field, value_cols=("ohc_700_se", "ohc_2000_se"))
    obs_cells = (
        ohc.grid_cells(profiles_df, list(DEPTHS), deg=1 / 12)
        if profiles_df is not None else None
    )

    if deg < 0.1:
        truth_monthly_all = truth_field_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().rename({"time": "month"})
        _bf = (gp_field[list(DEPTHS)] - truth_monthly_all[list(DEPTHS)]).rename(
            {d: f"{d}_bias" for d in DEPTHS}
        )
        bias_cells  = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(_bf, value_cols=[f"{d}_bias" for d in DEPTHS])
        truth_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_monthly_all)
    else:
        # coarsen_truth resamples+bins by each row's own date, so one call over
        # all three concatenated years already yields distinct per-year cells.
        truth_by_deg_all = ohc.coarsen_truth(truth_field_all, deg=deg)
        _merged = cells.merge(
            truth_by_deg_all[_keys + list(DEPTHS)].rename(columns={d: f"{d}_truth" for d in DEPTHS}),
            on=_keys, how="left",
        )
        bias_cells = _merged.copy()
        for _d in DEPTHS:
            bias_cells[f"{_d}_bias"] = bias_cells[_d] - bias_cells[f"{_d}_truth"]
        truth_cells = truth_by_deg_all

    return dict(
        cells=cells, se_cells=se_cells, obs_cells=obs_cells,
        bias_cells=bias_cells, truth_cells=truth_cells,
        obs_profiles=profiles_df,
    )

def build_gp_ctx(out_csvs, profiles_df=None):
    return {_GP_RES_LABELS[deg]: build_gp_res_ctx(out_csv, deg, profiles_df) for deg, out_csv in out_csvs.items()}

_real_ctx = build_gp_ctx(_real_out_csvs, profiles_df=real_argo_direct)




Format the pooled GP fit parameter table for display
def _fmt_fit_summary(df):
    d = df.copy()
    d["display"] = d.apply(
        lambda r: f"{r['estimate']:.4g}" if pd.isna(r["std_error"])
        else f"{r['estimate']:.4g} +/- {r['std_error']:.3g}",
        axis=1,
    )
    wide = d.pivot(index="parameter", columns="depth", values="display")
    order = ["mean (intercept)", "variance", "spatial_range", "temporal_range", "smoothness", "nugget"]
    return wide.reindex([p for p in order if p in wide.index])

EARTH_RADIUS_KM = 6371.0
_scale = {
    "mean (intercept)": ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, "variance": ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ ** 2,
    "spatial_range": EARTH_RADIUS_KM, "temporal_range": 1.0,
    "smoothness": 1.0, "nugget": 1.0,
}
_disp = gp_fit_summary.copy()
_disp["estimate"] = _disp.apply(lambda r: r["estimate"] * _scale[r["parameter"]], axis=1)
_disp["std_error"] = _disp.apply(lambda r: r["std_error"] * _scale[r["parameter"]], axis=1)
fit_table = _fmt_fit_summary(_disp)
fit_table.index.name = "parameter"
fit_table

title: Bounding Preferential-Sampling Bias in OHC
text:
Argo floats sample the ocean sparsely and non-randomly. In a region with a strong current and a sharp heat-content gradient – our North Atlantic Current (NAC) box \(A = [36^\circ,40^\circ]\,\mathrm{N}
\times [-68^\circ,-62^\circ]\,\mathrm{E}\) – floats are advected along the flow and may preferentially occupy waters whose ocean heat content (OHC) differs systematically from the regional average. Any estimate of regional-mean OHC built by interpolating float observations therefore inherits a sampling-induced bias. This report states, explicitly, the mathematics we use to place a rigorous upper bound on that bias.
The setting is unusually favorable because GLORYS12 gives us truth on a complete spatiotemporal grid. Let the region be discretized into \(N\) cells \(\{c\}\) (the native \(1/12^\circ\) GLORYS grid) with cosine-latitude area weights \(a_c \ge 0\), \(\sum_c a_c = 1\), and let \(Y_c\) denote the true cell OHC (per depth horizon \(0\)–\(700\) m or \(0\)–\(2000\) m, per month). The estimand is the true area-mean OHC over the region, \[
\mu \;=\; \sum_c a_c\, Y_c ,
\] computed in code by ohc.truth_domain_mean (cosine-latitude weighted, evaluated once on the native grid so it does not drift with cell size).
A float deployment realizes a sampled set \(S \subseteq \{c\}\) with per-cell profile counts \(n_c \ge 0\) (\(n_c = 0 \iff c \notin S\)). Two estimators appear below: the truth averaged over the sampled cells, \[
\bar Y_S \;=\; \frac{1}{|S|}\sum_{c\in S} Y_c ,
\] and an interpolated estimator \(\hat\mu\) (the Gaussian-process area-average of nac_gp.qmd, or a simple cell-bin mean). Because the truth is known, the bias of any realized sample, \(B = \hat\mu - \mu\), is directly computable – not merely estimable. “Proving an upper bound” thus means bounding a functional of a random, field-correlated sampling design, and then verifying the bound against the directly-computed truth bias.

title: Bounding Preferential-Sampling Bias in OHC
text:
3.1 A design-based covariance identity
Encode the sampling design by a normalized weight \(w_c \ge 0\) defined so that the sampled-cell mean is a reweighting of the population mean, \[
\bar Y_S \;=\; \sum_c a_c\, w_c\, Y_c ,
\qquad \sum_c a_c\, w_c = 1 ,
\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
w_c \;=\; \frac{\mathbb{1}[c \in S]\, / |S|}{a_c}.
\] By construction \(\mathbb{E}_a[w] \equiv \sum_c a_c w_c = 1\). Subtracting \(\mu = \sum_c a_c Y_c\) gives the identity at the heart of the preferential-sampling literature (the design-based view of Horvitz and Thompson (1952); the spatial formulation of Diggle et al. (2010)): \[
\boxed{\;B^{\mathrm{samp}} \;=\; \sum_c a_c (w_c - 1)\, Y_c \;=\; \operatorname{Cov}_a(w,\, Y)\; }
\] where \(\operatorname{Cov}_a\) is the area-weighted covariance. The preferential-sampling bias is exactly the covariance between the sampling intensity and the field. If floats sample independently of OHC, \(w \perp Y\) and the bias vanishes in expectation; bias arises precisely to the extent that the sampling intensity co-varies with the heat-content field.






NoteDerivation: the covariance identity





Start from the two means and use \(\sum_c a_c w_c = 1\) (so \(\mathbb{E}_a[w]=1\)) and \(\mu = \sum_c a_c Y_c\) (so \(\mathbb{E}_a[Y]=\mu\)): \[
B^{\mathrm{samp}} = \bar Y_S - \mu
= \sum_c a_c w_c Y_c - \sum_c a_c Y_c
= \sum_c a_c (w_c - 1)\, Y_c .
\] The area-weighted covariance, expanded about its means, is \[
\operatorname{Cov}_a(w, Y)
= \sum_c a_c\,(w_c - \mathbb{E}_a[w])(Y_c - \mathbb{E}_a[Y])
= \sum_c a_c\,(w_c - 1)(Y_c - \mu).
\] Distributing the \((Y_c - \mu)\) factor, \[
\operatorname{Cov}_a(w, Y)
= \sum_c a_c (w_c - 1)\, Y_c \;-\; \mu \underbrace{\sum_c a_c (w_c - 1)}_{=\,\mathbb{E}_a[w]-1\,=\,0}
= \sum_c a_c (w_c - 1)\, Y_c = B^{\mathrm{samp}}.
\] The cross term vanishes because the weights are normalized; no distributional assumption on \(Y\) or on the sampling is used, so the identity is exact for every realization.

title: Bounding Preferential-Sampling Bias in OHC
text:
3.2 A distribution-free hard cap (Cauchy–Schwarz)
Applying the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality to the covariance gives a bound that holds for any field and any design: \[
\big| B^{\mathrm{samp}} \big|
\;=\; \big|\rho_{w,Y}\big|\; \sigma_w\, \sigma_Y
\;\le\; \sigma_w\, \sigma_Y ,
\] with the area-weighted spatial standard deviations \[
\sigma_Y^2 = \operatorname{Var}_a(Y) = \sum_c a_c (Y_c - \mu)^2 ,
\qquad
\sigma_w^2 = \operatorname{Var}_a(w) = \sum_c a_c (w_c - 1)^2 .
\] Both are known: \(\sigma_Y\) is the spatial variability of the true OHC field, read directly off GLORYS; \(\sigma_w\) is the inhomogeneity of the sampling, read off the float density. The correlation \(\rho_{w,Y}\) is the only unknown, and bounding it by \(1\) gives the worst case.
The cap has a transparent closed form under a mild idealization. If the \(N\) cells are of (near-) equal area and the sampling intensity is roughly uniform over the \(|S| = f N\) sampled cells (so \(w_c = 1/f\) on \(S\) and \(0\) off it), then \(\sigma_w^2 = (1-f)/f\) and \[
\boxed{\;\big| B^{\mathrm{samp}} \big| \;\le\; \sigma_Y \sqrt{\tfrac{1-f}{f}}\;}
\] where \(f = |S|/N\) is the sampled fraction. The worst-case preferential-sampling bias is set by just two numbers – the spatial variability of OHC and how much of the region the floats cover.






NoteDerivation: Cauchy–Schwarz cap and the closed form for \(\sigma_w\)





Cauchy–Schwarz step. Treat \(a_c\) as a probability weighting. For any two vectors indexed by \(c\), \[
\Big| \sum_c a_c\, u_c\, v_c \Big| \le \Big(\sum_c a_c\, u_c^2\Big)^{1/2}\Big(\sum_c a_c\, v_c^2\Big)^{1/2}.
\] Applying this to \(u_c = w_c - 1\) and \(v_c = Y_c - \mu\) and using the identity above, \[
\big|B^{\mathrm{samp}}\big| = \big|\operatorname{Cov}_a(w,Y)\big|
\le \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}_a(w)}\,\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}_a(Y)} = \sigma_w\,\sigma_Y,
\] with equality iff \(w_c - 1 \propto Y_c - \mu\); writing the ratio as the correlation \(\rho_{w,Y}\) gives \(|B^{\mathrm{samp}}| = |\rho_{w,Y}|\,\sigma_w\,\sigma_Y\).
Closed form for \(\sigma_w^2\). Take equal-area cells \(a_c = 1/N\) and uniform sampling over the \(|S| = fN\) sampled cells. Then \(w_c = \mathbb{1}[c\in S]\,/(|S|\,a_c) = 1/f\) on \(S\) and \(0\) off it. Splitting the sum over sampled and unsampled cells, \[
\sigma_w^2 = \sum_c a_c (w_c - 1)^2
= \frac{1}{N}\Big[\underbrace{fN}_{c\in S}\big(\tfrac{1}{f}-1\big)^2 + \underbrace{(1-f)N}_{c\notin S}(0-1)^2\Big]
= f\Big(\tfrac{1-f}{f}\Big)^2 + (1-f).
\] Factor out \((1-f)\): \[
\sigma_w^2 = \frac{(1-f)^2}{f} + (1-f) = (1-f)\Big(\frac{1-f}{f} + 1\Big) = \frac{1-f}{f}.
\] Hence \(|B^{\mathrm{samp}}| \le \sigma_Y\sqrt{(1-f)/f}\). (Unequal cell areas or non-uniform intensity only change \(\sigma_w\); the general cap \(\sigma_w\sigma_Y\) still holds exactly.)



The amplification factor \(\sqrt{(1-f)/f}\) diverges as coverage \(\to 0\) and vanishes as coverage \(\to 1\):


Illustrative: the amplification factor (self-contained, no data)
f = np.linspace(0.01, 1.0, 400)
amp = np.sqrt((1 - f) / f)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.plot(f, amp, color="#1f77b4", lw=2)
ax.set_xlabel("sampled fraction  f = |S| / N")
ax.set_ylabel(r"amplification  $\sqrt{(1-f)/f}$")
ax.set_ylim(0, 6)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
for fv in (0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5):
    ax.annotate(f"f={fv:g}: x{np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv):.1f}",
                xy=(fv, np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv)),
                xytext=(fv + 0.03, np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv) + 0.3),
                fontsize=8, color="#444")
    ax.plot(fv, np.sqrt((1-fv)/fv), "o", color="#d62728", ms=4)
_show_fig(fig)



<div style="text-align:center">









Figure 1: Worst-case sampling-bias amplification factor sqrt((1-f)/f) as a function of the sampled fraction f. The Cauchy-Schwarz cap on |B^samp| equals this factor times sigma_Y, the spatial standard deviation of the true OHC field. Sparse coverage (small f) inflates the worst-case bias sharply.






</div>



This is the always-valid guarantee: it makes no assumption about how floats sample and cannot be violated. Its price is looseness – it is attained only if the sampling intensity is perfectly (anti-)correlated with OHC.

title: Bounding Preferential-Sampling Bias in OHC
text:
3.3 A sharper, data-driven bound (permutation)
To tighten the cap we ask how large \(|B^{\mathrm{samp}}|\) plausibly gets when the sampling pattern is not tied to the field. This is a randomization test with null hypothesis \[
H_0:\quad \text{the sampling pattern } S \text{ is independent of the field } Y .
\] We realize \(H_0\) by applying area-preserving isometries – rotations and reflections of the region, together with (toroidal) shifts – to the observed sampling pattern relative to \(Y\). Writing \(g_b\) for the \(b\)-th such transformation applied to the weights, we recompute \[
B^{\mathrm{samp}}_{(b)} \;=\; \operatorname{Cov}_a\big(w \circ g_b,\; Y\big),
\qquad b = 1,\dots,B .
\] Crucially, these transformations preserve the sampling pattern’s own spatial autocorrelation (clustering, coverage, geometry) while destroying only its alignment with the field. This is the standard remedy in tests of spatial association (Clifford et al. 1989; Mantel 1967): naive independent shuffling of cell labels would break the pattern’s autocorrelation and yield an anti-conservative – too narrow – null.
The randomization delivers two things:

Significance – a Monte-Carlo \(p\)-value for whether the realized bias exceeds what chance sampling geometry produces, \[
p \;=\; \frac{1 + \#\{\, b : |B^{\mathrm{samp}}_{(b)}| \ge |B^{\mathrm{samp}}_{\mathrm{obs}}| \,\}}{B + 1}.
\]
A tighter bound – an upper quantile of the null magnitudes, \(q_{0.975}\big(\{\,|B^{\mathrm{samp}}_{(b)}|\,\}\big)\), which typically sits well below the Cauchy–Schwarz cap because real sampling is far from perfectly correlated with OHC.

title: Bounding Preferential-Sampling Bias in OHC
text:
4.1 Linearity and the worst-case (minimax) bound
Both the interpolated estimator and the target are linear functionals of the field. The kriging predictor of the area-mean is \[
\hat\mu \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \omega_i\, Y(s_i),
\] a fixed linear combination of the \(n\) observations at float locations \(s_1,\dots,s_n\) with area-integrated kriging weights \(\omega_i\); the target is \(L(Y) = \mu = \int_A a(s)\, Y(s)\, ds\). Their difference is the residual functional \[
\Delta(Y) \;=\; L(Y) - \sum_i \omega_i\, Y(s_i).
\] Over an RKHS ball \(\{\, Y : \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}} \le R \,\}\), the worst-case reconstruction bias has a closed form (the minimax linear-estimation duality of Donoho (1994); the representer theorem, Wahba (1990)): \[
\boxed{\;
\sup_{\|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}} \le R} \big| \Delta(Y) \big|
\;=\; R\, \big\| \eta \big\|_{\mathcal{H}},
\qquad
\eta \;=\; \xi_L - \sum_i \omega_i\, k(\cdot, s_i),
\qquad
\xi_L(\cdot) = \int_A a(s)\, k(\cdot, s)\, ds ,
\;}
\] where \(\xi_L \in \mathcal{H}\) is the Riesz representer of the area-mean functional (\(\langle \xi_L, f\rangle_{\mathcal{H}} = L(f)\) for all \(f\)).






NoteDerivation: the residual functional is \(\langle Y, \eta\rangle\), and its worst case is \(R\|\eta\|\)





The one fact that makes everything linear-in-\(Y\) collapse is the reproducing property: for every \(f \in \mathcal{H}_k\) and location \(s\), \(f(s) = \langle f, k(\cdot, s)\rangle_{\mathcal{H}}\). Hence each point evaluation is an inner product, and so is the area-mean functional, by exchanging the (bounded) integral with the inner product: \[
L(Y) = \int_A a(s)\, Y(s)\, ds
= \int_A a(s)\, \langle Y, k(\cdot,s)\rangle\, ds
= \Big\langle Y,\ \int_A a(s)\, k(\cdot,s)\, ds \Big\rangle
= \langle Y, \xi_L\rangle .
\] This identifies the Riesz representer \(\xi_L(\cdot) = \int_A a(s)\,k(\cdot,s)\,ds\). Writing the point evaluations the same way, the residual functional becomes a single inner product: \[
\Delta(Y) = \langle Y, \xi_L\rangle - \sum_i \omega_i \langle Y, k(\cdot,s_i)\rangle
= \Big\langle Y,\ \xi_L - \sum_i \omega_i\, k(\cdot,s_i)\Big\rangle
= \langle Y, \eta\rangle .
\] Maximizing over the ball is then just Cauchy–Schwarz in \(\mathcal{H}\): \[
\sup_{\|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}} \le R} \big|\langle Y, \eta\rangle\big| = R\,\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}},
\] attained at the worst-case field \(Y^\star = R\,\eta/\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\) – the smoothest field of norm \(R\) that the observations cannot pin down.



The norm of the residual representer is computable in closed form from the kernel alone: \[
\big\| \eta \big\|_{\mathcal{H}}^2
=
\underbrace{\iint_{A\times A} a(s)\,a(s')\,k(s,s')\,ds\,ds'}_{\langle \xi_L,\xi_L\rangle}
\;-\; 2\sum_i \omega_i \int_A a(s)\, k(s, s_i)\, ds
\;+\; \sum_{i,j} \omega_i\, \omega_j\, k(s_i, s_j).
\]






NoteDerivation: the closed-form representer norm





Expand \(\|\eta\|^2 = \langle \eta, \eta\rangle\) with \(\eta = \xi_L - \sum_i \omega_i k(\cdot, s_i)\) bilinearly: \[
\|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}^2
= \langle \xi_L, \xi_L\rangle
- 2\sum_i \omega_i\, \langle \xi_L, k(\cdot, s_i)\rangle
+ \sum_{i,j}\omega_i\,\omega_j\, \langle k(\cdot, s_i), k(\cdot, s_j)\rangle .
\] Each inner product is evaluated by the reproducing property, \(\langle g, k(\cdot, s)\rangle = g(s)\):

\(\langle k(\cdot, s_i), k(\cdot, s_j)\rangle = k(s_i, s_j)\) – the Gram matrix of the observations;
\(\langle \xi_L, k(\cdot, s_i)\rangle = \xi_L(s_i) = \int_A a(s)\, k(s, s_i)\, ds\) – the mean-to-obs cross terms;
\(\langle \xi_L, \xi_L\rangle = \int_A a(s)\, \xi_L(s)\, ds = \iint_{A\times A} a(s)\,a(s')\,k(s,s')\,ds\,ds'\).

Substituting gives the boxed formula. Nothing but the kernel \(k\), the area weights \(a\), and the kriging weights \(\omega\) enters – no knowledge of \(Y\) is required to evaluate the bound.



This is exactly the kriging (posterior) variance of the area-mean functional. The radius \(R\) is calibrated from the data we already hold – the empirical RKHS norm of the GLORYS truth field, or equivalently the fitted process variance \(\sigma^2\) – giving the deterministic cap \(|B^{\mathrm{grid}}| \le R\, \|\eta\|_{\mathcal{H}}\).

title: Bounding Preferential-Sampling Bias in OHC
section: 4.2 Link to the posterior standard error already computed
text:
4.2 Link to the posterior standard error already computed
When the weights \(\omega\) are the kriging-optimal ones – exactly those our GP predictor uses – the residual-representer norm equals the GP posterior standard deviation of the area-mean, \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}} = \|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\). Hence the deterministic minimax cap is a known multiple of the posterior SE, \[
\big| B^{\mathrm{grid}} \big| \;\le\; R\,\|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}} \;=\; R\cdot \mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}},
\qquad R = \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}_k} \;=\; \rho/\sigma,
\] where \(\rho = \|Y\|_{\mathcal{H}_r}\) is the truth’s norm in the unit-variance correlation kernel and \(\sigma\) the fitted process standard deviation. That posterior SE is precisely what ohc_gp_predict.R::kriging_predict already returns and what nac_gp.qmd already turns into \(\pm 1\) SE and \(\pm 1.96\) SE prediction intervals. The existing \(\pm 1.96\,\mathrm{SE}\) coverage-rate calibration against truth is therefore precisely the honesty check for the probabilistic version of this bound: if the nominal intervals cover the truth at their stated rate, the posterior SE is a trustworthy (probabilistic) bound on \(B^{\mathrm{grid}}\); the minimax result upgrades it to a deterministic one at the cost of the single dimensionless constant \(R\).






NoteDerivation: why \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}} = \|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\)





Write the observation Gram matrix \(K_{ij} = k(s_i, s_j)\) and the mean-to-obs vector \(c_i = \xi_L(s_i) = \int_A a(s)\,k(s,s_i)\,ds\). The (noiseless) simple-kriging weights minimize \(\|\eta\|^2\) over \(\omega\); setting the gradient of the closed-form norm to zero, \[
\nabla_\omega\Big(\langle\xi_L,\xi_L\rangle - 2\,\omega^\top c + \omega^\top K \omega\Big) = 0
\;\Longrightarrow\; \omega^\star = K^{-1} c .
\] Substituting back, \[
\|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}^2
= \langle\xi_L,\xi_L\rangle - 2\,\omega^{\star\top} c + \omega^{\star\top} K \omega^\star
= \langle\xi_L,\xi_L\rangle - c^\top K^{-1} c .
\] This is identically the Gaussian-process posterior variance of the linear functional \(L(Y)\) given the observations, \[
\operatorname{Var}\!\big(L(Y) \mid Y(s_1),\dots,Y(s_n)\big)
= \underbrace{\textstyle\iint a\,a'\,k}_{\langle\xi_L,\xi_L\rangle} - c^\top K^{-1} c ,
\] so \(\mathrm{SE}_{\mathrm{post}} = \|\eta^\star\|_{\mathcal{H}}\). (A nugget/observation-error variance \(\tau^2\) simply replaces \(K\) by \(K + \tau^2 I\) throughout, adding the usual measurement-noise term to the posterior variance; the identity is otherwise unchanged.)

title: Argo Bias
text:
This site collects analysis reports for a study of ocean heat content sampling bias in Argo float observations.
The sampling bias arises because Argo floats are not uniformly distributed in space or time. We use GLORYS12 reanalysis fields and PARCELS Lagrangian particle simulations to characterize and quantify the bias.
Use the sidebar to navigate between reports and jump to individual sections.

title: Argo Bias
text:
This site collects analysis reports for a study of ocean heat content sampling bias in Argo float observations.
The sampling bias arises because Argo floats are not uniformly distributed in space or time. We use GLORYS12 reanalysis fields and PARCELS Lagrangian particle simulations to characterize and quantify the bias.
Use the sidebar to navigate between reports and jump to individual sections.

title: NAC Targeted Analysis
text:
1 Goal and design
TODO


2 Setup
This report targets the same Gulf Stream / North Atlantic Current analysis region as pilot_simulation.qmd, but compares the GLORYS truth OHC field against the RG Argo Climatology across three years (2020, 2021, 2022) rather than just one. 2020 velocity and temperature come from data/velocity_gs_wide/ (lat 30–48\(^\circ\)N); 2021 and 2022 come from data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/ (lat 33–45\(^\circ\)N, downloaded by code/download_gs_2021_2022.py), a tighter latitude band over the same longitude span and GLORYS12V1 product. Both cover the analysis region with room to spare.


Setup — imports and experiment configuration
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath("../code"))

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import xarray as xr
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

import ohc
import ohc_bias
import ohc_climatology as ohc_clim
from trajplots import map_fields_row

YEARS = [2020, 2021, 2022]

# --- per-year velocity/temperature source and its download domain ---
VELOCITY_GLOB = {
    2020: "../data/velocity_gs_wide/velocity_2020_*.nc",
    2021: "../data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/velocity_2021_*.nc",
    2022: "../data/velocity_gs_2021_2022/velocity_2022_*.nc",
}
DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS = {  # (lat0, lat1, lon0, lon1) -- as downloaded, differs by year
    2020: (30, 48, -74, -49),
    2021: (33, 45, -74, -49),
    2022: (33, 45, -74, -49),
}

ANALYSIS_BOUNDS = (36, 40, -68, -62)     # float deployment box (4$^\circ$ lat x 6$^\circ$ lon)
CONTEXT_BOUNDS  = (34, 42, -70, -60)     # ANALYSIS_BOUNDS + 2 deg margin, inside every year's domain
EXTENT_WIDE     = [-76, -47, 28, 50]     # cartopy map extent for the wide-context maps
DEPTHS          = ["ohc_700", "ohc_2000"]   # both depth layers, shown throughout




3 Analysis region
The analysis domain sits on the Gulf Stream / North Atlantic Current front — a sharp north–south OHC gradient. The annual-mean GLORYS OHC below shows high OHC (~90 GJ m\(^{-2}\)) to the south and low OHC (~45 GJ m\(^{-2}\)) to the north, for each of 2020, 2021, and 2022.
OHC definition. For every 1/12\(^\circ\) grid cell, OHC (GJ m\(^{-2}\)) is the integral \(\text{OHC} = \int_0^{z} \rho\, c_p\, T\, dz\) computed using the trapezoidal rule for \(z = 700\) m and \(z = 2000\) m where \(T\) is the sea water potential temperature in \(^\circ\)C reported at 50 standard depth levels (41 levels from 0m-2250m: 0.41m, 1.54m, …, 644m, 763m, …, 1942m, 2225m). Unlike Levitus et al. [2012], which computed \(\rho\) and \(c_p\) from annual climatological T/S fields, here both are fixed constants – adequate at this study’s scope (a \(4^\circ\times6^\circ\) region and a bias between two estimators computed the same way, so most of the T/S-dependence common to both cancels) but worth revisiting if the analysis later targets absolute OHC magnitudes or multi-year trends, where Levitus-style climatological \(\rho,c_p\) would matter more.


Load per-year GLORYS velocity/temperature and compute the true OHC field
# Cache truth_ohc_field (reads all 41 depth levels -- the dominant cost here)
# to NetCDF so re-rendering doesn't repeat it. Delete the .nc to force a recompute.
OHC_TRUTH_DIR = "../data/ohc_truth"
os.makedirs(OHC_TRUTH_DIR, exist_ok=True)

theta = {}
truth_field_full = {}
for y in YEARS:
    la0, la1, lo0, lo1 = DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS[y]
    theta[y] = xr.open_mfdataset(VELOCITY_GLOB[y]).sel(
        latitude=slice(la0, la1), longitude=slice(lo0, lo1),
    )
    cache_path = f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_ohc_{y}.nc"
    if os.path.exists(cache_path):
        truth_field_full[y] = xr.open_dataset(cache_path).load()
    else:
        truth_field_full[y] = ohc.truth_ohc_field(theta[y]).compute()
        truth_field_full[y].to_netcdf(cache_path)



202020212022




Code
_ = plot_ohc_field_year(2020)








Figure 1: GLORYS 1/12\(^\circ\) 2020-mean OHC (0–700 m and 0–2000 m)









Code
_ = plot_ohc_field_year(2021)








Figure 2: GLORYS 1/12\(^\circ\) 2021-mean OHC (0–700 m and 0–2000 m)









Code
_ = plot_ohc_field_year(2022)








Figure 3: GLORYS 1/12\(^\circ\) 2022-mean OHC (0–700 m and 0–2000 m)








Argo floats in this region park and drift at ~1000 m between profiling cycles. The annual-mean uo/vo near 1000 m below shows the strong North Atlantic / Gulf Stream current a float deployed here would be advected by, for each year.

202020212022




Code
_ = plot_velocity_1000_year(2020)








Figure 4: GLORYS 2020-mean horizontal velocity near the 1000 m parking depth.









Code
_ = plot_velocity_1000_year(2021)








Figure 5: GLORYS 2021-mean horizontal velocity near the 1000 m parking depth.









Code
_ = plot_velocity_1000_year(2022)








Figure 6: GLORYS 2022-mean horizontal velocity near the 1000 m parking depth.










4 OHC Anomaly
Climatology source. data/rg_climatology/RG_ArgoClim_Temperature_2019.nc (the RG Argo Climatology) contains ARGO_TEMPERATURE_MEAN: a single static mean temperature field (Jan 2004 – Dec 2018, no seasonal/monthly dimension) on a 1\(^\circ\) lat/lon grid and 58 pressure levels (2.5–1975 dbar). Its longitude is 0–360\(^\circ\)E and is converted to -180..180\(^\circ\) on load.
The twelve monthly extension files RG_ArgoClim_<YYYY><MM>_2019.nc each contain the observed ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY from the 2004–2018 mean for each calendar month. We load these for each of 2020, 2021, and 2022.
Climatological OHC field. Either mean-temperature field is integrated over depth with the same trapezoidal rule used for GLORYS (ohc.profile_ohc, treating RG’s pressure in dbar as meters, the same approximation already used throughout this report), giving an ohc_700/ohc_2000 field on RG’s native 1\(^\circ\) grid.
Linear interpolation from 1\(^\circ\) to 1/12\(^\circ\). Because the true GLORYS temperature field is on a 1/12\(^\circ\) grid, code/ohc_climatology.py bilinearly interpolates the climatological OHC to the 1/12\(^\circ\) grid for each calendar month.
Anomaly. For a grid cell at \(x = (x_{lat}, x_{lon})\) observed in month \(t\) of year \(y\), the OHC anomaly is calculated by subtracting the spatially interpolated climatological OHC for that calendar month (built from that year’s RG extension file) from the observed monthly-mean GLORYS OHC value. This is repeated independently for 2020, 2021, and 2022, each against its own year’s RG climatology anomaly.


Load the RG climatology (seasonal, per year + static) and compute the truth OHC anomaly for 2020-2022
mean_da = ohc_clim.load_rg_mean()
clim_field_B = ohc_clim.climatological_ohc_field(mean_da)  # static 2004-2018 mean

clim_field = {}       # per-year seasonal climatology (Option A), month dim 1-12
truth_field = {}      # per-year truth field restricted to the analysis region
truth_anom = {}       # per-year GLORYS truth OHC anomaly (native 1/12$^\circ$)
truth_anom_cells = {}
for y in YEARS:
    ext_da = ohc_clim.load_rg_extensions(year=y)
    clim_field[y] = ohc_clim.climatological_ohc_field(mean_da + ext_da).compute()
    truth_field[y] = truth_field_full[y].sel(
        latitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1]),
        longitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3]),
    )
    truth_anom[y] = ohc_clim.truth_ohc_anomaly(truth_field[y], clim_field[y])
    truth_anom_cells[y] = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_anom[y])

# truth_field[y]'s spatial grid is identical across years (same ANALYSIS_BOUNDS
# slice of the same native 1/12 deg grid) -- only the time axis differs -- so
# concatenating along time lets downstream monthly-mean/coarsen calls run once
# on all three years instead of once per year.
truth_field_all = xr.concat([truth_field[y] for y in YEARS], dim="time")

# All three years concatenated -- one 36-month table for the multi-year maps/limits below.
truth_anom_cells_all = pd.concat(truth_anom_cells.values(), ignore_index=True)

# Publish both to data/ so nac_gp.qmd (and any other downstream report) can load
# them directly instead of re-deriving them from the raw RG climatology files.
truth_field_all.to_netcdf(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_field_analysis.nc")
truth_anom_cells_all.to_csv(f"{OHC_TRUTH_DIR}/truth_anom_cells_all.csv", index=False)

# Shared diverging colour range for every *anomaly* map below, across all years.
ANOM_VLIM = {d: float(truth_anom_cells_all[d].abs().quantile(0.98)) * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ for d in DEPTHS}


Below is comparison of the RG climatology published temperature trend and my recreation



Published RG Climatology global-mean temperature time series (source: sio-argo.ucsd.edu)




Reconstruct published RG global-mean temperature time series (2004–2026)
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
from pathlib import Path

_ds_rg  = xr.open_dataset(ohc_clim.RG_MEAN_PATH, decode_times=False)
_mean_v = _ds_rg["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_MEAN"].values    # (pres, lat, lon)
_anom_v = _ds_rg["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY"].values # (180, pres, lat, lon)
_p      = _ds_rg["PRESSURE"].values
_lat    = _ds_rg["LATITUDE"].values
_ds_rg.close()

_dp     = np.diff(_p)
_coslat = np.cos(np.deg2rad(_lat))

def _vol_mean_T(T):
    T_mid = (T[:-1] + T[1:]) / 2    # trapezoidal averages; NaN where either endpoint NaN
    w     = _dp[:, None, None] * _coslat[None, :, None]
    return float(np.nansum(T_mid * w) / np.nansum(np.where(np.isnan(T_mid), 0.0, w)))

_dates_main = pd.date_range("2004-01", periods=180, freq="MS")
_tmean_main = np.array([_vol_mean_T(_mean_v + _anom_v[i]) for i in range(180)])

# Load all available extension months (2019 onward)
_ext_targets = [(y, m) for y in range(2019, 2027) for m in range(1, 13)
                if not (y == 2026 and m > 2)]
_ext_dates, _ext_vals = [], []
for _y, _m in _ext_targets:
    _path = f"../data/rg_climatology/RG_ArgoClim_{_y}{_m:02d}_2019.nc"
    if not Path(_path).exists():
        continue
    _ds = xr.open_dataset(_path, decode_times=False)
    _anom = _ds["ARGO_TEMPERATURE_ANOMALY"].values.squeeze(0)
    _ext_vals.append(_vol_mean_T(_mean_v + _anom))
    _ext_dates.append(pd.Timestamp(_y, _m, 1))
    _ds.close()

# Bridge the Dec 2018 / Jan 2019 gap by prepending the last climatology point
# to the extension series so the two line segments share an endpoint.
_bridge_date = pd.Timestamp(2018, 12, 1)
_bridge_val  = _tmean_main[-1]

_all_dates = np.array([_bridge_date] + _ext_dates, dtype="datetime64[ns]")
_all_vals  = np.concatenate([[_bridge_val], _ext_vals])

_smooth  = pd.Series(
    np.concatenate([_tmean_main, _ext_vals]),
).rolling(12, center=True, min_periods=12).mean().values
_sm_dates = np.array(list(_dates_main) + _ext_dates, dtype="datetime64[ns]")
_sm_ok    = ~np.isnan(_smooth)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
ax.plot(_dates_main, _tmean_main, color="black", lw=0.8, label="2004–2018 (mean + anom)")
ax.plot(_all_dates, _all_vals, color="red", lw=0.8,
        label=f"2019–{_ext_dates[-1].year} extensions")
ax.plot(_sm_dates[_sm_ok], _smooth[_sm_ok], color="limegreen", lw=1,
        label="12-month running mean")
for y in YEARS:
    ax.axvspan(pd.Timestamp(y, 1, 1), pd.Timestamp(y, 12, 31), color="#4a90d9", alpha=0.06)
ax.set_ylabel("Potential Temperature ($^\circ$C)")
ax.set_xlim(_dates_main[0], _all_dates[-1])
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator(2))
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%Y"))
ax.grid(alpha=0.2)
fig.tight_layout()








Figure 7: Verification: global-mean RG potential temperature reconstructed from mean + monthly extension anomalies. The ~0.04 \(^\circ\)C upward offset vs the published plot above is expected — the RG website excludes marginal seas and the Arctic from its spatial average; we include all non-NaN cells.







RG climatology (static), native 1\(^\circ\) grid vs interpolated to 1/12\(^\circ\)
_clim_B_interp = ohc_clim.climatology_on_grid(
    clim_field_B, truth_field_full[2020]["latitude"].values, truth_field_full[2020]["longitude"].values
)

_STATIC_MAP_KW = dict(
    titles=["ohc 700m", "ohc 2000m"],
    cmaps=["viridis", "viridis"], cbar_labels=["GJ m$^{{-2}}$", "GJ m$^{{-2}}$"],
    extent=EXTENT_WIDE,
    boxes=[(*DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS[2020], "download domain", '0.5', "-"),
           (*ANALYSIS_BOUNDS, "analysis domain")],
)

_ = map_fields_row(
    [clim_field_B["ohc_700"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, clim_field_B["ohc_2000"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ],
    suptitle="RG climatology (native 1$^\circ$)", **_STATIC_MAP_KW,
)
_ = map_fields_row(
    [_clim_B_interp["ohc_700"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, _clim_B_interp["ohc_2000"] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ],
    suptitle="RG climatology (bilinear -> 1/12$^\circ$)", **_STATIC_MAP_KW,
)











(a)











(b)






Figure 8





RG climatology by year and month, native 1\(^\circ\) grid vs interpolated to 1/12\(^\circ\), the GLORYS truth, the GLORYS truth - RG climatology anomaly, and eastward/northward velocity
# Fixed view for every panel below, regardless of its native grid resolution.
ANALYSIS_XLIM = (ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3])
ANALYSIS_YLIM = (ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1])

_clim_A_plot_years, _clim_A_interp_cells_years = [], []
for y in YEARS:
    months_y = [pd.Timestamp(y, m, 1) for m in clim_field[y]["month"].values]
    plot_y = clim_field[y].sel(
        latitude=slice(CONTEXT_BOUNDS[0], CONTEXT_BOUNDS[1]),
        longitude=slice(CONTEXT_BOUNDS[2], CONTEXT_BOUNDS[3]),
    ).assign_coords(month=months_y)
    _clim_A_plot_years.append(plot_y)
    _clim_A_interp_cells_years.append(
        ohc_clim.climatology_cells_table(
            clim_field[y], truth_field_full[2020]["latitude"].values, truth_field_full[2020]["longitude"].values,
            months=months_y,
        )
    )

# One 36-month table per map kind, across all three years.
_clim_A_native_cells = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(xr.concat(_clim_A_plot_years, dim="month"))
_clim_A_interp_cells = pd.concat(_clim_A_interp_cells_years, ignore_index=True)

# GLORYS truth, monthly-mean, same grid as the anomaly. truth_field_all already
# carries real per-year dates, so one resample over all three years gives
# correctly-dated months directly -- no per-year loop/relabeling needed.
truth_monthly_all = truth_field_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().rename({"time": "month"})
truth_monthly_cells_all = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(truth_monthly_all)

# Velocity near the 1000 m parking depth, monthly-mean, analysis region only.
# theta[y] itself can't be concatenated (different years cover different lat/lon
# extents -- see DOWNLOAD_BOUNDS), but restricting each to ANALYSIS_BOUNDS first
# gives identical grids that can be, so the resample below still runs once.
_theta_analysis_all = xr.concat(
    [
        theta[y][["uo", "vo"]].sel(depth=1000, method="nearest").sel(
            latitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[0], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[1]),
            longitude=slice(ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[2], ANALYSIS_BOUNDS[3]),
        )
        for y in YEARS
    ],
    dim="time",
)
velocity_monthly_all = _theta_analysis_all.resample(time="1MS").mean().compute().rename({"time": "month"})
velocity_cells_all = ohc_clim.to_cells_table(velocity_monthly_all, value_cols=("uo", "vo"))
VEL_VLIM = float(pd.concat([velocity_cells_all["uo"], velocity_cells_all["vo"]]).abs().quantile(0.98))

# Shared absolute-OHC colour range across native/interpolated climatology and truth.
_abs_bounds = {
    d: (float(pd.concat([_clim_A_native_cells[d], truth_monthly_cells_all[d]]).mul(ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ).quantile(0.02)),
        float(pd.concat([_clim_A_native_cells[d], truth_monthly_cells_all[d]]).mul(ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ).quantile(0.98)))
    for d in DEPTHS
}

def show_clim_map(kind, d):
    cmin, cmax = _abs_bounds[d]
    common = dict(width=TABSET_FIGW, ncols=6, xlim=ANALYSIS_XLIM, ylim=ANALYSIS_YLIM)
    if kind == "native":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            _clim_A_native_cells, d, vmin=cmin, vmax=cmax,
            title=f"RG climatology {d} by year/month (native 1$^\circ$)", **common)
    if kind == "interp":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            _clim_A_interp_cells, d, vmin=cmin, vmax=cmax,
            title=f"RG climatology {d} by year/month (bilinear -> 1/12$^\circ$)", **common)
    if kind == "truth":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            truth_monthly_cells_all, d, vmin=cmin, vmax=cmax,
            title=f"GLORYS truth {d} by year/month (native 1/12$^\circ$)", **common)
    if kind == "anom":
        return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
            truth_anom_cells_all, d, vmin=-ANOM_VLIM[d], vmax=ANOM_VLIM[d], cmap="RdBu_r",
            cbar_label=f"{d} anomaly (GJ m$^{{-2}}$)",
            title=f"GLORYS truth - RG climatology (native 1/12$^\circ$)", **common)

def show_velocity_map(component):
    label = "eastward" if component == "uo" else "northward"
    return ohc_bias.plot_monthly_cell_maps(
        velocity_cells_all, component, vmin=-VEL_VLIM, vmax=VEL_VLIM, cmap="RdBu_r",
        value_scale=1, cbar_label=f"{component} (m s$^{{-1}}$)",
        title=f"GLORYS {label} velocity ({component}) ~1000 m, by year/month",
        width=TABSET_FIGW, ncols=6, xlim=ANALYSIS_XLIM, ylim=ANALYSIS_YLIM,
    )

_emit("::: {.panel-tabset}")
for d in DEPTHS:
    _emit(f"## {DEPTH_LABEL[d]}")
    _emit("::: {.panel-tabset}")
    for kind, label in [("native", "Native 1$^\circ$"), ("interp", "Interpolated to 1/12$^\circ$"),
                         ("truth", "GLORYS truth"), ("anom", "Anomaly")]:
        _emit(f"### {label}")
        _show_fig(show_clim_map(kind, d))
    _emit(":::")
_emit("## Eastward velocity")
_show_fig(show_velocity_map("uo"))
_emit("## Northward velocity")
_show_fig(show_velocity_map("vo"))
_emit(":::")

Code
def _box_mean(field, bounds=ANALYSIS_BOUNDS, value_cols=DEPTHS):
    la0, la1, lo0, lo1 = bounds
    sub = field.sel(latitude=slice(la0, la1), longitude=slice(lo0, lo1))
    w = np.cos(np.deg2rad(sub["latitude"]))
    return sub[list(value_cols)].weighted(w).mean(dim=("latitude", "longitude"))

box_by_year = {y: _box_mean(clim_field[y]) for y in YEARS}  # dims: month (1-12), per year
box_B = _box_mean(clim_field_B)  # scalar, static 2004-2018 reference

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4), sharex=True)
months = np.arange(1, 13)
palette = plt.get_cmap("tab10").colors
for ax, d in zip(axes, DEPTHS):
    for i, y in enumerate(YEARS):
        ax.plot(months, box_by_year[y][d] * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, "-o", color=palette[i], label=f"{y} mean")
    ax.axhline(float(box_B[d]) * ohc_bias.J_TO_GJ, color="0.4", ls="--", label="static 2004-2018")
    ax.set_title(f"{d} background OHC, analysis-region mean")
    ax.set_xlabel("calendar month")
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.grid(alpha=0.2)
axes[0].set_ylabel("OHC (GJ m$^{-2}$)")
axes[0].legend(frameon=False, fontsize="small")
fig.tight_layout()
